title: Saltimbocca alla romana
subtitle: Roman chicken escalopes with prosciutto and sage in white wine
excerpt: Thin chicken breast escalopes laid with a slice of prosciutto and a sage leaf, secured with a toothpick, then fried in butter and deglazed with white wine into a brief pan sauce. Fast, elegant, and entirely correct — a 15-minute Roman dinner-party dish.
sourceNotes: Saltimbocca alla romana (jump-in-the-mouth, Roman-style) is one of the classic dishes of Roman trattorias and is traditionally made with veal escalopes. This version uses chicken breast, which is widely available, similarly thin when butterflied or beaten, and works equally well with the prosciutto and sage combination. The dish appears in early twentieth-century Roman cookbooks and is attributed to the cooking of the Lazio region.

content.text: The escalopes must be thin — 5 mm at most. If the chicken breast is thick, butterfly it by cutting horizontally through the middle and opening it flat, then beat it between sheets of cling film with a rolling pin or meat mallet until even and approximately 5 mm thick. A thick escalope will not cook through in the time the prosciutto takes to crisp.

Cook prosciutto-side down first. The prosciutto crisps against the hot butter, the fat renders, and the sage wilts and flavours the butter. The whole first side takes 2 minutes. The second side takes 1 minute. The pan is then deglazed with white wine that reduces in 2 minutes into a thin, savoury jus that is poured over the escalopes at the plate.

items.prepNote: butterflied and beaten thin to approximately 5 mm
use sparingly — prosciutto provides most of the salt

content.text: Place a slice of {{prosciutto}} on each {{chicken-breast}} escalope, then lay a {{sage}} leaf on top and secure with a cocktail stick through all three layers. Season the chicken side with pepper only. Heat the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large frying pan over medium-high heat until foaming. Place the escalopes prosciutto-side down and cook for 2 minutes until the prosciutto is crisp and the sage is fragrant. Turn and cook for 1 minute on the chicken side. Remove to warmed plates and remove the cocktail sticks.

Pour the {{dry-white-wine}} into the hot pan and let it bubble, scraping up the base, for 2 minutes until reduced by half. Pour the jus over the escalopes and serve immediately. Crusty bread or briefly sautéed spinach alongside.

items.symptom: Prosciutto has fallen off and the sage has separated
items.cause: Cocktail stick not securing all three layers tightly
items.fix: Pierce the stick through the sage leaf first, then through the prosciutto, then down into the chicken so all three layers are pinned together

items.symptom: Chicken is still raw in the centre after 3 minutes
items.fix: Beat the chicken to 5 mm; if it was left thicker, finish in a 180°C oven for 5 minutes after the pan

items.symptom: Pan sauce is too thin and watery
items.fix: After adding the wine, keep the heat high and let it bubble vigorously for a full 2 minutes; it should reduce to a syrupy consistency

items.cause: Prosciutto was heavily cured and more salt was added
items.fix: Do not season the chicken with salt — the prosciutto provides all the salt needed; taste the jus before pouring

content.text: Saltimbocca alla romana is a trattoria staple in Rome and the surrounding Lazio region, and one of the dishes that defines the character of Roman cooking: quick, unapologetic about fat, made with high-quality cured meat, and finished with wine rather than cream. The name (jump in the mouth) reflects the expectation of the dish — it should be so good that it imposes itself. The version with chicken rather than veal is widely made in Rome today, and is indistinguishable from the original in everything except the provenance of the meat.
